speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Frances Vaughan Fry took place at her home in Kendrick on February 18, 1977. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Heavy snows, but you can't tell people, a lot of people around here, you can't tell these people of the snow we used to have. We used to have, well it wasn't anything to have three or four feet. And then of course it would, oh well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of times it would, you know, blow and what do you call it? Drifts?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well we'd have to go and get them drifts out of the way and everything before we could get out. And I saw the times that we lived about a half a mile from the main road up on Cedar Creek there. And we had to, the men folks that had a bobsled, you know, we had the double bob, big bobsled. And they just took the front bobsled and made a bob out of them, was coming to Kendrick. And they started down the lane and the men folks had to go ahead of the horses and break the trail so they could get through. Because it was the deep. And, but I don't know what winter it was. It was a long time ago. But we, by land we showed, we never thought anything was shoveling out our road. And then one year we had snow till way up in May. And there was a man that run out of hay, didn't have any hay for his stock. And the snow had crusted. And everything could go on top of the snow. The horses and the cattle and pigs and everything was out. Everybody's cattle was out all over the country. Everybody had everybody else's cattle. And they drove right across, they would come into our place off of the main road. They come in kind of slant ways to the barn. They drove right in there with the bobsled and got a load of hay and drove right back out over the tops of the fence and everything. Now people won't believe you. Some of these young hanyaks around here, they won't believe you, they think you're just talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hanyaks?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, hanyaks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, that's what we call some people. And they just won't believe things like that. Why it wasn't anything. We went, one time we went for, one of my oldest son, I think was just a baby. Now that's been 60 some years ago. We went over to our neighbors and they had to go down about three quarters of a mile or possibly a mile down to their place. And we had company and we were in the bobsled and the men folks had to go, and it took us over three hours to go that distance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: About, what was about possibly a mile. Oh my. Down the lane and they had to go ahead and break the road for the horses so the horses could get down there with the sled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would they break the road with just walking along?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, they'd go ahead and the horses you see first and then the horses would come along.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Of course if the horses had to break the road but the horses would leap, you know, jump like that. But when they walked this, you know, and made the trail and then the horses could walk along. Things like that. But we've done that many a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much time, say, in the winter that you would not go any place? You'd just have to stay at home and wouldn't even try to stay at home?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, of course they'd be days that we wouldn't get out for maybe a day or two. But then the neighbors, oh, you see, we lived within possibly, well, it was a mile.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Some was mile and a half, mile and a quarter, mile or something like that or three quarters of a mile. We'd get out and shuttle out our road to the main road. No, we'd done that lots of times.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I wonder what's happened. It's changed the cold snow pattern so much that now we don't have the snow in the winter that we used to. Oh, no, nothing like it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: In that one winter, we had lots of snow too that winter. And that was the winter. I don't remember what winter was. Other people could tell you the exact winter it was. But we had down now, we lived on one side of the neighborhood up there. We lived on the side out this way farther. Our neighbor lived back farther towards the Boulder Creek country. And it was always 10 degrees colder over at his place. We had 30, around 30, and he had 40 degrees.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Was that cold?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, the stock and all the animals and everything we had to make. Of course, then we used to thrash and have big stacks of straw. Those cattle would go over there and just eat in, burrow under, and eat in. When they didn't have a shed, we had shed for most of them. But then there was times that we didn't have shed for all of them. The rest of them would go there around these piles of straw and just get as near in as they could under that straw. And stuff was so cold. But that was the only winter that I remember that was so, we've had cold weather. And since we've been down here, we've had some cold weather because my daughter and I used to walk from, we lived out where the schoolhouse is and how we lived out there. We were working, both of us working down here, and we'd come down the railroad track. We used to walk down the railroad track, and it was, well, lots of times it was 20 below when we walked down the railroad track.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far were you walking into town?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, from out before those two houses out there, into town. And we came down the railroad track. And there was just lots of mornings. Now, that 20 degrees didn't mean too much if there was no wind, if it was just quiet. But all if there was a wind, you just couldn't, you're free today. And that's the way it is, of course, now. But then we've had a few cold days this, even this winter we've had a few cold days. But this is nothing like we used to have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to have to worry about the stock getting too cold in the winter?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No. There was no problem. No, we had barns and chairs and all that. They could some way get kind of out of the cold. We had a big cold barn and a horse barn.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then, of course, we had our hog pens and all houses for them. And all, no, the stock was pretty well taken care of as far as that was concerned. Of course, our hay was in the barn. And we could just feed them right out. But we never had any trouble. We had to maybe shovel our path to the barn like that. And I know one, the night that it was so often cold up there, during this time, that it was so often cold. So many people lost all their chickens. Their chickens just froze right on the roof, or right in the coops. And I know our neighbor, my neighbor lady, she had built a new chicken house. And one of those real cold nights, one of my children were born. And I just fussed all night long because I thought, well, my chicken's a priest today. Now, I'll tell you what, I had my chickens in, and that was an old building. Well, it was a pretty good size shed. And they roasted in this old shed. It wasn't a real chicken house. They roasted in this shed. But they was up here, right at the top. You know, it was built like this. The roof was down like this. And then there was pieces up here at the top. Well, those chickens was all up on that up there. And I never lost a frozen chicken. But this lady that had that new chicken house lost hers. Because there was so much moisture up there with the chickens in there. There was so much moisture in there that they had one of them froze. Now, that's unbelievable, maybe, but that was sold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because it was built too tight? It was built tight?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And chickens, I guess they kind of sweat or something like that. And it just froze. But then there was a lot of people that lost them. They had regular chicken houses, and they lost their chickens. But these of mine was up there in the top. They didn't froze. And I was worried because I knew they weren't fed and they were taking care of them that evening.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how come there's somebody else in the family?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, they were there, but then nobody else did. That's the chickens for me.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So they didn't get any attention. That's not to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that how it was straightened out like that? That you had certain things you did, and those were only you did them?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, they were things that they did, but other people didn't think about it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It wasn't there. There, well, Mr. Fry wasn't any hand to do chores as far as asking to do. And I managed the chores and things like that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That's what I was thinking. I mean, I got the idea that a lot of farm families that there were certain chores that the wife did, certain things that the husband did, and it was always set up. There was a lot of that too, but then it was just like the milking. I always did the milking.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had about five or six cows most of the time. I did the milking for one of the boys, got big enough to help me. Then one of the boys helped me and one of them helped their dad. I had two boys pretty close together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that take very long to milk six cows?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I don't know. Maybe. I looked through the gimbal anywhere from a half to an hour. We had some good cows too. We had some good kids. We had a cow, and there's not very many people that have a cow like that. We had one cow that we raised the family with almost. She was a big cow. She weighed, well, when we sold her, she weighed 1 ,800 pounds. And she gave the best milk that was ever invented of that. And the children could tell if I put any other milk on the table, but this cow's milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd say, what's the matter with the, what was her name?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Muley, I believe you'd call her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter, Muley?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had a, well, at the time, we had charthorns and we had jerseys and we had this, she was a big roll. Meanest thing that ever walked on four legs. But she was the best cow you ever saw. And one time there was a fella from up there to work.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And when she first came fresh, she lost her first calf, and we didn't know she'd lost it. The neighbor had bought her. She was a calf that we had sold to this neighbor.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And so when she came fresh, she was down in the canyon. This neighbor never got her. And something got her calf. Well, he didn't know it, which was too late, and he just let her go. So that before she caved the next time, we bought her back. It was one year, it was a year of election, and we were betting. Each one of us was all betting on this or a president and that president and somebody else. And everybody was selling something or buying something at that time, and we bought this cow back. She was a cow then, and she was refreshing that spring. Well, she did. She freshened. And she was the biggest thing, and she just gave the most milk. And all to her. I don't know. I swore up and down my husband was no handy. He wouldn't milk, you believe. He just wouldn't milk. Well, sometimes he had to depend very seldom. He'd always milk. I had a little jersey, and he'd always milk this little jersey, and I'd have other cows milk. But this old big cow, she was the one that we depended on for many years. And so she had her calves. Well, she had... She'd never been milked. Of course, they'd never milked. And once she had the first calves, they'd never milked her. So when we got her and she had the calves, we never milked her. We just put three calves on her. We let three calves on her all summer. And she nursed these three calves. And then when winter time come, we needed some milk, a cow milk. Our other cows, I guess, were dried up. So we had a hired boy there with me, and he went to the... It was when they was having the fairs down at Lewisden there, the regular fairs, they always had horses and cows and hogs like that. He went to the fair, and he took these three calves off of this cow. And my land, it wasn't for the day, but this cow couldn't hardly walk. She heard, she had a big bag, and she didn't hardly walk. So I told this boy, I said, Well, I said, if you go with me out to the barn, I said, I'll try to milk that cow. Well, by the time he got back, I was milking the cow. And she was just real... She was kind of a, you know, nervous cow. And you couldn't change milkers on her. She didn't want anybody else to milk her. But the one that was used to milking her, like, I've milked her all the time, possibly that I could. And no one else did. So I milked her for years. But that cow, about once a month, she would kick. And we tried to put these kickers on her, but they weren't over. She just, they just went everywhere. So I'd just have to milk her, and if she kicked me, I'd have to take it. And I'd take it. Well, we had had a man there that did try to milk her without, you know, the kickers, and he went out there. And every time he went out to the barn, we could hear him fighting that cow. He would whip her, and he did everything he could do. And so finally I told my husband, I said, I said, I'll milk her. So I got to milk her. So I milked her all the time. But once in a while, when she took the notion to kick him, she didn't kick to miss you. She kicked to hit you. And you usually got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, sir. And she gave, I'll tell you what she gave, it was before your time. And I don't suppose you ever saw that. Square cans of cola that we used to come to town to get a five -gallon can of cola for our lamps and things, you know. They were about that big square. And all they, well, they held over five gallons. Well, we, after the oil was out of them all, we cleaned them up good. And that's what I took for that cow's milk. I had a small bucket that I milked it. And then I had this big bucket. And I'd milk many a day, day after day, and day after day every time I milked that cow, I got that five -gallon bucket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Every day?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Every day, twice a day. That five -gallon, damn it. And our neighbor was up to our place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I went out to milk one morning, and he says, well, what are you going to do with all those buckets?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I said, I'm going up to the cow. Well, he wouldn't believe it. And things like that. Well, I did it, just the same. And we kept that cow, well, just, we kept her for years and years and years. Oh. And finally she had twin calves. And then we had to get rid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: She was, well, she just didn't do it good, you know. We had to just let her go. But she sure was a good cow, and she was a cow. Now, with the Jersey, I had a separate, a cream separate. With the Jersey cow, you could run her milk through the separator. Well, when you got the milk through the separator, there was nothing left in it. It was just nothing but milk, just milk, you know. It was no cream in it at all. Well, this old cow's milk, you could separate it, and you could skim it. And the milk would, or cream would raise on it always. Now, that was the kind of milk she had.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Now, you know, you said when you got that cow there, it was during an election, and everybody was betting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, did you win that cow on a bet, or did you?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, no, we were just betting. I know we bet somebody bet you a coat, or somebody bet you this, or they bet you that. And I think that was the year that I had three steers.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It seems to me like I bought those steers. My husband had, they were his, that is possibly his. Well, I bought those three steers for my own dudes, and he bought this cow. That's how we had the cow. He bought this cow back from this neighbor, and I bought these steers from him. And one steer I sold for over $100. One steer died, and let's see now what happened to the other. I got $100 out of one, and one died. And something happened to the other, and that's what, with my money that I bought those steers from. And he still had his cow.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And always, it was a great life. I used to do those things. And I forget, I don't just remember, my brother won a coat, a Mackinac coat, from somebody that year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was he betting Democrat or Republican that year?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I'm the president. I'm wondering when. Oh, let's see.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I don't even. Oh, it doesn't matter. No, I don't remember that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He won a Mackinac.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, he won a Mackinac. And then there was just quite a lot of, you know, they bet money. Money in those days was money. It didn't act much. So we had money. When the different ones get the money, you know, look at that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: You know, one thing about milking cows, now I've never done that, you know. I mean, it's almost past my time already to just be milking cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know some people still do, but when you went out to milk a cow like her, what kind of method did you use? I mean, did you take just a real direct approach and not fool around at all?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, we had them in stanchions. We had the cows in stanchions.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Every cow knew where she brought them. But we had them in these stanchions, and then we'd usually feed them. And then, of course, the other cows, we tried. There's a thing they call a figure eight. Well, a lot of times we'd put just a figure eight on the other cows, but this one we tried. We had these, it was a thing that you hooked over one leg and crossed and hooked over the other leg. Well, she just kicked the thing off the pieces. You couldn't put them on her. She wouldn't let you. She'd just fight and just kick. All the time you couldn't do a thing with it. So I just quit trying it with them on her. And I learned, I used to always, when I sit down to milk, I had my milk stew in my bucket, you know. I'd sit down to milk and I'd put my head in the cow's flank. If it's a small cow, you can put your head in the cow's flank and she can't kick you. Maybe if she tries to kick, she can't raise her leg because I had my head in there. And I tried that with this cow, but it didn't work. She pretty near broke my neck. So I didn't try that anymore on her. I just have to sit there and just pray that she wouldn't kick you. Every time I let out there, milk, because she never knew when she was going to kick my end stew.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So that was that one.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But it wouldn't pay to try and hit her.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, no, that's what they'd done in the first place. They'd say, whip her. They just took a raw hide, you know, one of them old black snakes and just all this man that was working for us. He was going to whip it out of her, but he never whipped it out of her. And it just made her worse. You just touch her with anything, you know, and whip her while that doesn't even work. And she's one of these cows. She never bothered you. She never done a thing to you. You could go out anywhere among her, go around her. She wouldn't kick you when you just walked around her and around where she was and things like that. But she was a cow that she was out in the gravel or out in the field or out in the pasture. You know, some of the cows, you can kind of make a run at them and they'll take off like they're shot. But this cow, she never moved. You couldn't scare her. She wasn't afraid of nothing. You just make a run of her and she'd come right back at you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think cows like that are fairly smart animals?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I mean, I've heard people say that cows are just dumb. And I somehow, I have the idea that they're not as dumb as people think they are.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had two or three cows. We got several cows from where we went over to Harvard and got a couple cows.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: They were kind of, well, it was my parents' cousin. And we went and got these and we called them fat and yellow. They were good cows. And I could take my milk peels and start for the barn. And the cows would be back to we'd harvest. We'd always turn the cows into fields, you know. And I'd holler, you know, call kind of to them, Pat and Al, Elva. And here they come. Well, this one would try to beat the other one there and she'd come right up to them. And I could sit down and milk her any place. She's the two. And I could milk her right up out in the corral, you know. And I'd just take my book and stool and go up and sit down. Whichever one got there first, a lot of them. And wanted to try to beat the other one. Oh, yes, they're smart. And the same thing about when they go into their stalls. They had certain stalls. And one of them get in the wrong place and you just call them the name. And boy, out of there they get and get older. They knew where they belonged. You didn't have to know, know the cows. I think they're just smart as anything else if you try to teach them anything. Because we had, well, anything around our place that I ever knew of, the horses and cows, pigs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We could do most anything, wouldn't we?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: You know, you were talking about having a hired man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a hired man much of the time helping down the place?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Not only in the summer, just for summer work. Only when someone maybe had wanted a place to stay. A lot of times they'd be, some man had been working, didn't have no place to stay, and he'd come and he'd say, well, I'll be chores for, get bored and bed.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, there'd been people do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be a neighbor man or would it be somebody to tell you what you were doing?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, it'd be someone we knew, mostly.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then we did have a hired man quite a little bit in the spring. And for a long time, children got big enough to help. That we had a hired man in harvest doing my fetch all the time. But otherwise, I wouldn't have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he work with your husband mostly? Was that what he would do? Would he just work alongside of him?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, he'd have his, he'd maybe take care of the horses or run the teams out in the field or whatever they were doing, lying or whatever they were doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: You want to do one thing, you want to do another.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then he used to keep the, help me with the garden and different things like that. Whenever he was, didn't have something else to do. But then that was in the time when we paid him then, you see. But in the wintertime, when there was nothing, practically nothing to do, why only just chores, why he, we'd just have them there, you know, to do chores. Then sometimes Mr. Fry was away from home. He went one year, he was up in the woods, up hauling lumber from the siding to, or from the sawmill later on in Boulder Creek, over to the siding that, where the train come along on the track, you know. I'm picking one of them. He was up there hauling lumber, different things. And was away from home. And this boy would stay there with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you had a hired man stand, was they just like a member of the family?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh yes, they'd come in and sit down at the table, and he was just like the same as anybody else.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: In the hire stands, that was a great time when we had the hire stands. We just, all the men, premier, that was on the harvest crew, well we'd have, before they had such a thing as, they had horses and wagons, you know, to haul the hay, or the bundles into the fashion machine. And like that, and then we'd have the engineer and the man that hauled the water, and the man that did run the machine, and the one that run the, when the horses went around on that, you know, there was a certain team. Right. That hooked onto this, what you call it, a sweep. It went around and around and around, and that's what run the engine, or the machine over here. We'd have anywhere from 20 to 30 men. That was a big time. Of course we didn't have too much to thrash, just a day or two, but that was all. They'd go to someplace else, and each woman would go and help the other woman with the crew, when she had a crew, and things like that. We all helped each other in the harvest, and everything. Like putting up ice, and we were talking the other day, we used to always put up ice, you know, why the ice would be that thick. Just, and all we'd cut that ice, because we had an old log cabin, and we had sawdust in it, and had for years, and we'd put that down in the sawdust. We had ice all summer. One neighbor would help the other neighbor, and they'd cut this ice and haul it to these places, and cover it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is this log cabin just for your own supply, or was this?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We'd have put it in there, and a lot of people would run the water over it and let it all freeze in one big chunk, and we had ice all summer. You could take that ice, and like now you get a piece of ice and wrap it up into something, and it isn't any time that it's gone. Well, we used to get those chunks about like that, and we had old, maybe old blankets, or old something like that, and we'd wrap them up there. Well, we could keep an ice chunk for a week, but we can't do that anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you cut the ice, and then they have parties going in, and off the creek there?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, we had ponds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Ponds.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then one time they could drive right out on the, you know, they started in the pond, and then they could drive right out there and load up, and the time or two, the teams went in the fog. They had to cut the harness off and everything else to get them out. You know, they went in there. They couldn't, the horses couldn't get loose, and they just had to get in there and cut them loose. But, like, it was great to be, and we skated and everything on these ones. I should be, well, it'd be that big square, you know, and that thick. Oh, just a big square. And they'd wrap it over the sauce and cut it off. One way would help the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that the women, farm women, men worked as hard as men did?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I always have said the women work harder than men, wherever they are, and they do today. Because a man has so many hours of working. And when he's ours, it's up, he's through. But it will, if you're a family, like we had, we had the children, we had the meals, we had everything else to do, besides helping them there. Until my young, one of my oldest boys was 12 years old. I went right out in the field and shot the grain behind the binder.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I was, when my boy had all that tickle in my voice, they thought that was just why they said, Mama's not going to help us anymore. And so I just quit, and the boys went on from there. But I helped in the field, besides taking care of the family and all like that. I helped in the field. I do my milk and stuff like that, all of a sudden. I don't know how I done it, but I did. Never thought anything about it, because most women, they didn't do all that. They didn't work outside, and they didn't do a lot of things, nor they didn't do chores, nor they didn't milk cows, or anything like that. But I did.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Most of the neighbors even didn't. Yeah, I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you different, you think? What made you be like that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, we just thought it was necessary, I guess. Because we didn't have anything else. We just had to make what we could on anything we could, and we just had all this stuff, just to be done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, they all had pretty much the same thing to do, didn't they?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, it was according to whether they had cattle or horses, or what they had. They had to take care of what they had, but they didn't allow them. They didn't have a lot of things to do, other than just, you know, their chores was very short. But we always had plenty of chores, because we had cattle, and we had dogs, and everything. The children, I took care of the dogs and cows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that your family was maybe more ambitious, and that they were trying to do more, had more things?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, they helped to have more things, but just like it was with our family. We taught our family. As they grew up, we had chores for them to do. Now that's what's the matter.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: There's no, why they don't even, there isn't a child I'll bet in this town that knows what it is to carry in wood, or to get wood, or to, oh, they was just different chores to be done. And, well, they wouldn't know more, you know, how to go out and feed a horse, or a cow, or anything. They wouldn't have no other thing about it. Well, if we went someplace, if Mr. Fry and I went someplace, I nearly always would take the youngest one with me. And then the rest of them, Grandma was there, and when they got old enough, they'd be there, and be there for the day. We never did leave nights very much, but we was gone sometimes, but Grandma was always there with them. So whenever we went away, if we come home late at night, those chores were all done. Our children done them. They never done anything else. They went ahead and done the milking, and they attended to the stock, and put the stock in, and fed everything. They had to be fed night and morning, and everything like that. There was nothing to it. They didn't know anything else. Then when they had time, maybe they'd go out and saw wood, or maybe they'd go do this or do that, and they had their horses. Later years, they all had ponies and things like that to ride, and if they was, oh, the kids wanted to go someplace, they'd jump on their horses, and away they'd go. They'd go fishing, like that, you know, around. But they had their time the same as we did, and that was it. But they knew how to work. One of my kids would know how to work, and they'd know how to cook. If there's any one of them who can cook, they can do anything there is to do. Always has been that way. The girl's the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's because you expected it of them from when they were little?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I had it to do. When they were little, my oldest boy, he knew what it was to take care of the younger ones, because he took care of the younger ones. I had the other work to do, and I did the main part of taking care of them. But then when I got them cleaned up for the day and all like that, he'd take them outside and play, and we'd put them to a nap, you know, and things like that, and he'd have that kind of a play. But a lot of times he had the children, all these kids with him. I had eight. And the children would go with him, and I'd go ahead and do the work. But when they went any place then, after they got older, while they went different places, they wouldn't get home at chart time, something like that, I always did the chores for them. And they always did them for me, whenever I was done. So there was no question about it. There was no question that. No, we didn't have to tell them. If we didn't get home, the chores were done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, on the average day, would you be helping your husband in the field? On the average day, or just something special like shocking?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, no, I did lots of outside work, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what else would you be doing?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, well, I don't know. Just numerous things that, it was to be done. We'd have the shocking, and then we'd haul in hay, and then I'd turn, we used to have to turn our shops, we used to have to turn the beans, we used to raise beans, and the beans would get wet, and we'd have to turn them. And all of that stuff, I helped with all of that. And cutting the, well, we took one year, we had seed, seed clover, and it was late, you know. And oh, it got cold before we got it all cut. Well, I run the mower, rode the mower, and rode the team, and Mr. Fry went along and raked it so it would get on there so we could gather it, and things like that. All those little chores like that, I did. Along with his, you know, him, too. We both worked out with this bean deal while then. We used to be aware of the beans out of turning them over as rain and rain, and then we'd think we'd get to thrash them, and then rain some more. Oh, dear, like some of us had just talked, fork turns them and they'd jump over. And things like that. Well, there's lots of funny things that we had like that happen. You know, that didn't happen. We always laughed about it and said, it was good, and one year I took the children, and the beans that year, when they cut them, and they cut them and they shattered so bad that they was just, oh, just beans, just lots of beans in the rows. And that year we didn't have beans. And our neighbor, now he says, if you and the children want to come over and pick up, they was pods and they was beans. And we could pick up all we wanted to while we went over and picked up many sacks of beans. Just in these rows where it was left, he wasn't going to do anything with them because he wouldn't pick them up. You couldn't rake them up or anything, you just had to pick them up. And there was pods and everything, and we picked the children up. Got out of them. How many sacks and things like that. And we went out, we picked potatoes one year. We helped man up there that put in potatoes, and we went and helped him with them. And when the children got big enough, they went there for a week or so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have beans that year that you picked his beans?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No. You didn't grow any. No, we didn't have any of that year. And he said, well, if you want to go over here to school, you can pick all you want. You can pick them as long as you want to, and so we went over there and picked by them. I'm still trying to figure why most women didn't do the kind of work that you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, those farm families, they were in the same situation pretty much, weren't they?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, but then they didn't do those things. They just didn't do it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: There's one or two women that done just the same as I did. One woman that didn't have any family. Oh, she helped her husband like that all the time. But then most of them, they just kind of sat around and didn't, some of them. Now, I never, my children never went dirty any time. I always managed some way, and the same way, they never always had the best in the world. But I never let them go to town or any place. Of course, they never went to town very often. But I never let my children go. But for many years, I lived close to my mother, and she helped me on that deal. She would help me mend the boys' pants, you know, things that they wear out there. Knees and stuff like that. Well, if I didn't get it done, she'd come down and help me with that. Same way with my stockings. I never let my children go any place. You know, some people let their children go with great holes in their stockings, and you saw children like that, and you saw them, because there's some of them coming down now like that. Well, I never let my children go like that. I mended the doors closed. I said, well, a patch was better than a hole any time. And I mended my children's clothes, and everybody else's, as far as asking to. Because we had it to do. There was nothing else we could do.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Now, these women that would sit around more, you know, I know that in the earlier days, I've heard a lot of talk about how sickly a lot of women were. They had a hard time with their health.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that some of what was going on with some of these women?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: They wouldn't be too well. Some of them were, but some of them just didn't do it. That was all. They just, whether they weren't sick, they just didn't do it.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I had many a neighbor, just the lovely neighbors, but they never done those things. They just never done it. They never mended their children. They let their children just go their pants and all, you know, just all ragged and everything, and they never done anything about it. Well, I did that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Of course, I didn't always keep it up, because sometimes I was too busy to do that at the time, but I did, and I caught up later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these women socialized a lot?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Do you think they would spend a lot of time, you know, Oh, we'd go, well, yes, we'd go and spend the day with one another and things like that, and have a jolly time if we were doing something special, and, well, just like maybe this woman would have a half a dozen chickens to dress or something.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, this other woman would come over and spend the day and help with these, and we'd get them out of the way. And things like that. Yes, we spent many a day, a pleasant day, with each other. But we do, and if they had something special, maybe they'd have a quilt top. Well, they'd get it ready, and we'd go visit them, and we'd tie the quilt, or we'd do whatever they had, whatever. If they was cleaning house, we'd go and help with the painting. We used to paint, you know, we used to take newspapers, any kind of newspapers we used to save, all of our newspapers, and any kind of newspapers we'd save, just like with these papers like these, they won't be this big. Papers like that, my mother always straightened them out, laid them out, and in the screen, we'd re -paper our house, and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the papers?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: With these papers, and things like that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Sometimes we had a newspaper, but it was very seldom we ever had a paper as big as the commune, or anything like that. We had these kind of papers, but not a newspaper big enough, you know. But we saved every paper, and then when it comes time to clean house, we'd go help somebody else, and they'd come help us.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But I'm thinking of like the wasting time that some of these women did. I mean, they couldn't watch the soap operas on TV, because they didn't have no TV. They probably didn't have radio either. I wonder what they did when they, instead of the work they maybe should have been doing. Well, I'll tell you, this is my work, but I do all of this stuff, you know, like for the children, all of that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And I've made bedspreads, I've made around 30 bedspreads for my grandchildren, my own children, and my grandchildren. And I'm making pillowcases now for my great -grandchildren. And all of those things, I've been busy at that. If I wasn't busy in the field, I was busy at something like that in the house. I didn't. But a lot of people would just sit down and just talk. They'd never done anything. Only just get their media or say, oh, we should have come to three games a day. One man demanded, one of our neighbors demanded he would be in the biscuits. So his wife always had to make biscuits for every meal. So one day, and they had lots of sickness in their face. So one time he was quite a fella to talk and tell things. So we used to have these dinners on Sunday. We usually had our, oh, if we had a picnic or somebody's birthday or something, we'd all gather. And Easter was one day we had, a big day. And we'd all take things, you know, and then we'd all cook. Everybody, of course, would bring the best they had. And this man, he, well, this is what I started telling him about these biscuit deals. Well, they didn't have a heating stove. They had their kitchen on one side of the, well, I guess they did have a heating stove in the second room. But on one side of a breezeway, they had their kitchen. Well, on the other side was their bedrooms and stuff like that. And he had all of his wood, he had to cut shorts, you know, for the old fourteen or sixteen inch long wood, you know, then split it for the foot stove. So one day he was sitting there, and his wife was one of these, if ever was an angel on her, this woman was one of them. She was the best person I ever knew in my life. I mean, she was the sweetest and the best that I ever knew. Her and it was another neighbor the same way. And she was never any hand to complain. Everything was just all right and nothing was going on. Every once in a while, she, maybe Mr. Langdon would start in and he'd tell a story or something. And then one day he was sitting and he was going on about it. He says, I'd just like to see piled up in the pile. He says, I'd like to see the wood piled up in the pile that I have split for that stove. No, he went on about splitting this wood. Had to split this wood. You see small wood to go in the stove. And after he got all through going on, Mrs. Langdon says, yes, Jim. She says, I'd like to see piled up in the pile all the biscuits that I baked in that.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And tickled, oh, it just tickled us all so. And she just laughed and she laughed and she got another good one on him like that. She didn't laugh about it, but then she just told that. And she just calmed the cucumber about it, but she just, and it just kind of shut him up for a while. Then when we'd go to these big pigments, he was always going on about this one or that one or something. And he ate, well, this was just delicious. That was just delicious. Oh, he said to us one day when we was out to one of these pigments, she had baked cherry pie, his wife had. And boy, he was just talking about, oh, that was just such delicious cherry pie.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Would it be all right if he had another piece of that pie?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But he just kept on talking about it and talking about it. Because she said, well, Jim, she says, that's the way I baked it for the last so many years since they'd been married. Why did she bake the cherry pie and things like that tickled us all because she never would say anything. Well, just once in a while like that. We all knew him so well on everything that we thought was pretty funny once he'd get off something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that made her such an angel to you?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I mean, it made her such a fine person. This Mrs. Langdon.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I never heard, I don't remember of every hearing, and we had another neighbor at the same time she was there. We had another neighbor. That I never heard that neighbor ever say anything bad about anybody. You could be talking to either one of them, and we'd, of course, I say my say if I don't like somebody or they do something, well, I'd say what I want to say. But it wouldn't make any difference. Some of us would be talking about some of these neighbors doing so and so and this and that and the other. And whatever was their fault, whatever we could see was their fault and all like that. These women, could each one of them always say some part, something of these people, some good of. Never, it never went any farther. It was always something good. If they couldn't say something good, they didn't say anything tall. And they could always do that. And whoever was some of them that were real, renegades, you know, while we'd be talking about them, but these women, they always could say something nice about these people that we talked about. And you know, that's something most people don't do. That is, don't say or don't do or don't stay by is to see something good in everybody. No matter what they did, it didn't make any difference. If they did that, well, it was something else they could say a good about. That's the reason I say these two women, if there ever was a Christian on earth, it was one of them.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And what I can't see through is one thing, and I guess we never can, any of us can. Why those two women had to suffer. Oh, how they did suffer before they passed on. Well, just something terrific. And I can't understand that. So I don't know why the reason was that those women were two of the, I think they were the, they weren't the best, maybe, but they were two of the best that I ever knew. And I never expected to know anybody that was as sweet as those two women.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It sounds like Mrs. Langland took a certain amount of guff from her husband too. Oh, she did. She never crossed him. She never said anything. I don't think whatever he'd done, why that was done, she never or anything.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And I know his daughter one time. They moved, finally moved from Cedar Creek down to Julietta. Somebody said something to the girl and he, well, he wasn't, he was one of the assessors up here. He was the one that was the one that was the most important for him. And something was in about him having money. And this girl, she says, well, she says, I think he's rich. But that was about her dad. She says, I think he's rich because he always kept everything he got. And so that's what I heard that part of it. And he was the, he was the great guy.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: All these, all of these experiences and things that we went through. And those kids were scared to death of him. When we were kids, we were scared to death of this man. We, they had Jordan saying, hey, Jimmy and I, we used to go and help him plant beans and things like that. But we surely scoot out of the way when he showed up. We didn't like him too well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he, did he show a temper to the kids? To you kids?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No. I never ever saw him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But his kids out here, you right now, they, what was to work?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Boy, they had to do the work. That was all it was to him. Now, I never asked my children, any one of my children. I never asked my children to ever do anything that I wouldn't do myself. If it come right for me to do it, I could do it myself. But I never asked my children to do it. When I wouldn't do it, if they had to do something and I could help them along with it, I'd be right with the side of it. And there's several men that I know. This man, I'm talking to Jim Langdon, his boy. Now, he made his boys work. He had boys later on. And he made his boys work. But that man worked right along the side of them boys. He never asked them to do this or do that just because it was a hard job to do. He worked right along with them. And I did the same thing with my children. I never asked them children. I never told him that he had to do something that I wouldn't do myself.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But the old man, the old man, he was more that way. He'd expect the kids to do things he didn't want to do. He'd tell the kids to do this or do that and do something that he wouldn't do himself. He never did.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He never did.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, it's funny, you know, it sounds like some of these men, even like your husband somewhat too, were kind of finicky about what they do and what they wouldn't do.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: The hell they were.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That's good. There's lots of things, I suppose. There's lots of things maybe that I should have done that I didn't do. But then at the same time, I figured, well, I never figured that I didn't. I did my part in raising the family and having the family. And I don't ever think that I ever infringed on his. When he wanted to go, he'd go. And he was always fishing and he was always doing this and he's always doing that. Well, when I had a baby in my arms, I didn't want to go fishing and not like that or take my children out like that because it was too much trouble to watch them when I was on the river or any place to watch those children. And I figured they had to be watched because they weren't used to it. And I was afraid they'd get, you know, grounded or something. Well, I figured that that was more trouble than to have them at home because they could go any place they wanted to their home. They could go and play. They usually had an old horse they could have or something like that. And they were busy and the kids would come in to see them or they'd go to see some of the kids and they were happy at home. And I said, I'm not going like that until I haven't the children. So I never went too much, but he went and way went to California and he fished. He went every year fishing and hunting and all of that stuff. So one of the neighbors, my neighbor's up there, she said, her husband took an ocean to go hunting with him a time or two. So she said to me one day, she said something about her husband. Now, when he came home and she hadn't done something that he figured, she didn't do it like he did, see. And when he came home, he kind of grumped about it. So she said to me, she was a dear friend too. And she said to me one day, she said something about, she said something about Clarence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he to say that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I said, no. I said, he don't. I said, he hasn't better either. Because I said, that's something. I said, when he goes away, I do those chores and things that he done all the time. I do them the best I know how and the best I can under the circumstances and everything. I do them. I says, if he has anything to say, he can stay home and do it. I'm not going to. I wouldn't.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So really, it sounds like, I mean, when you had kids, then he really had more kind of more freedom to come and do as he pleased. He did. He just did. He went and he come and he got what he wanted. And his mother, he was the only, well, they had some other children, that is, his mother.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He had a couple of stepsisters and two brothers, but they were grown and he was the baby. And he had everything he wanted. His folks gave him everything he wanted and anything he wanted. And he had everything he wanted. There's a lot of things come along, as long as the money lasted, he got it. But when the money was gone, then that was a different proposition. Somebody else had to kind of plan on that. So I just didn't, I done what I could. And as I say, when he was gone, and like I used to go out and cook for these, the crews after the children, I took my three kids with me. And I went up, I was up to Clarky. I was up to, up between here and, and the, you know, Pune and Kimber. And just different places. I went and took my three children. Well, I got the women's wages. Whatever they were paying their women, they always paid a man cook a lot more than they ever paid a woman cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And why?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It always was that way. I didn't get paid, the, I didn't get paid as much as the lowest man in the crew got for just what he did. And I was up from, anywhere from four o 'clock in the morning, till after nine at night. Getting my things straightened so I could cook. Of course, I had my three kids with me. I got my, I got my board along with it. And I considered that quite a little bit with my three kids. I had them with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of camps were these? Would these just be small camps in the woods?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes. Loggers and haulers and everything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men in a camp like this?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, in the one up at Breakfast Creek that I cooked for up there, I had 30.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But I did have a helper there. Then the missus, the man's wife, she'd come up a lot and she had children just the same as mine. Well, she used to come up and help me too in the summer. But I was there a lot of the times just for myself. Now I baked my own bread. And I made all of my sweets up. And the boss told me, now he says, you make pie and you make cookies and you make all of this sweet stuff. He says, when you're doing that, why that's taken off of something else. Well, he provided. He provided everything. He was the best guy you ever saw to work for. And we had to, we had to have hotcakes. Well, we had all kinds of cold cereal. And if there was some of them in the crew, we had to have some hot cereal. I made hot soup. We fried three and four dozen eggs every morning. And we had hotcakes and cookies. But I had a helper right at meal time. They called him...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Flunky?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Flunky. But he was a, well, he worked out on the roads then when he wasn't helping me. If I needed him, I called him because he'd help me do anything. He'd be a potato. He always fried the eggs every morning.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then I'd do the rest of it and we'd have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was making more money than you two?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, you had all that responsibility. I had all that responsibility, every bit of it. But this man that furnished, why he'd get, you know, the men had to make their lunch. They made their own lunch. I fixed the stuff and they made their lunch. Well, he'd get those big rolls that need this, you know, that big bowl that they use as much now. That's what the men liked was that. Well, any other kind of meat they didn't care for. Sometimes he'd get a chunk of it, but so many of them didn't like it that he got most of this. And we had the hams and bacon and shoulders and every kind of meat we could get. And we got fresh meat whenever we could. But when we were way up there in the timber, he would come to town maybe twice a week and get groceries. And he'd always get fresh meat when he came, but we couldn't always keep fresh meat. We didn't have no place to keep it. So it was always this other meat that we had. But he was not, and sugar, he got a sack of brown sugar, white sugar, any kind of sugar on water. We had all the flour and everything there was to do with. But then it was kind of a job to make bread for all of them. And in later years we bought bread. But for a lot of years you had to make the bread for their sandwiches and everything, you know, took all the bread.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have to watch the kids at the same time as you were working?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, yes, that was right.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah. That was the... We lived right by the river, but then the kids were thinking up at the time I was there. But when we'd go fishing or something like that, we'd go up on the river up here on the... you know, up the river. And there's such deep holes. Well, there was deep holes there, and the children got used to it. Well, after they got used to it, they could play wherever they wanted to. And they were out on the team, where most of the time that is, out around camp, they never went too far away from camp. But they were always out there. If she was up there with her kids, she had her for her, and they were all out together.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: The kids were, I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how hard of work was that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Was that really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: were you working most of the time from morning till night?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, if I got my work around, I could sometimes have a little time in the afternoon. A little breather, but then it wasn't much of the time I was busy most of the time. You know, doing something, if I wasn't doing something, I was baking bread or making cake or baking pie or something like that. We had pie and cake and cookies, all of them, for the nate. Some men will take when they fix their lunch, they'll take mostly cookies and stuff like that. And then others will take other things. But mostly they like the cookies and cake and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were these lumberjacks like? Were they real polite, courteous?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I never had to ask a want, not want, man, to ever... that he wasn't doing what he should be or anything like that. And one thing that they were very curious about and was very nice about, when they got up from the table, they would clean up their... around their plate, put everything on their plate, and bring that to my work table. And then all I had to do was scrape it off and put it in my pants. But they'd always clean their plate and bring it and set it on my table. And I didn't have to go to the tables and get it up there. And then this man that I had to help him with, he would come in, but I never had him come in very many times because I didn't need him. And I didn't have him because I had the whole day, and the men took their lunch, you see. I didn't have to fix him. But I didn't have to have breakfast and supper. But let's see, they'd come in around 6 o 'clock, I guess, at night, something like that. And then we got up and they were off to work by 7 in the next morning and things like that. So it wasn't too bad. I don't know how a villain would have done it. I couldn't do it now. I couldn't even think about doing it. But I sure could do a thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a cabin to live in or?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I lived in the back end of the cookhouse.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had a big cookhouse. And big long tables. There was two big long tables in the cookhouse, and then I cooked right in the corner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids slept there with you?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, we had kids back in there if they wanted to. Some of the boys, they'd put up a tent. They'd put a platform back of the cookhouse. And they had an extra tent on that. And if we had extra, they'd go back there to sleep. And then they had the big bunkhouses and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did you get home when you were doing that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, sometimes about every two weeks. I came home.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I stayed overnight and went back the next day. And it took us about four or five hours. And then we drove. And I went in with him. Sometimes I went in. We had to stop between here and get him back to camp. We had to stop and stay overnight. One night we stayed with a man up here. He was an old bachelor. He asked us to come in. And it happened. It rained that night. And he asked us to come in. And we all went in with our sleeping bags and everything. And he just turned the front rooms over to us. And all of us just bedded down right in that front room. But we had all the horses and all the equipment and everything. And we had started again. And we didn't get it. We stayed there. And then we went on in the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you took the horses?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Right with us. And we were just traveling with the whole crew was going in.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: That was after we was situated up. See, we went back and forth up here up to Breakfast Creek for several years. And was up there. And of course then we'd go in. Then we'd come out in the fall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do when you got home just to stay overnight? Did you have much work to do at home?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I didn't do much, no.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Because Mr. Fry was working too. He was away from home. So I didn't have much only just for ourselves. And then after later years, why then he was home. And he just cooked for himself. But I used to work around here. And anything I could get to do I had at that time. I cooked in most of the stores around here. And then I went out with a doctor just lots and lots of times. And went on cases, baby cases with him. And I stayed with the people for about 13 days, each one. And like that I used to go out like that too. But then the children were open up at home. And then they could just make it all right at home without me. And then later on when Mr. Fry was home, but then he was home while none of the children was ever home with him because they were either working or the youngest ones were from him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This going out with the doctor, did you do that very much?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I did for a good many years.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I wasn't a nurse. I never took any nurses training. But I got in with the doctor. And whenever he'd have a case maybe that they couldn't find anybody to go and he'd come get me and I'd go with him. And then I'd stay. Oh, they'd have to go with him. You know, usually at that time why they figured that a woman should stay down for 13, 12, 13 days. And I'd get that besides mine going out with the doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you still there?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Quite a bit. There's quite a few of the kids around here that I took care of. When they were little I had my picture taken with them. The other day we'd laugh so about. He's got a kid now. And so we had a picture taken together. I said to his dad after he was born, I said, I told his mother, I said, well, I said, that was the, he was the ugliest baby I ever saw. His dad would sort me for quite a while afterwards. And he said to her, he says, I don't think he's ugly. But he was a great big baby, long. And so poor, he just looked like a little bird, you know, that is his hands. So I told him, and this man, and then I teased him. And after I found out it made him kind of sorry, then I teased him the other time I'd seen about it. He's gone now.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: This little fellow that I took care of, he's around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pay did you get for staying in a place for almost two weeks like that?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, I got anywhere from home. I, five, ten dollars maybe for staying with him or something like that. Not too much. Because I did, well, most of them were people that, you know, that just really couldn't afford anything more. And I never asked anything more but I'd go and say, oh, I stayed with Albert Craig's and Lomond Sub -Tone Ridge. Oh, I went different places and I took care of the many of my daughters when they had their babies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you help the doctor do the delivery too?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I was there. I took care of my, the one time I was, I went, our neighbors up there raised turkeys. And this, they lived over on, on Boulder Creek, you've been Boulder Creek. Well, these people lived over at Boulder Creek. And they were to have this baby. Well, he started to Kendrick with his wife. His wife was taken, you know, she was time for a baby to come. And they got as far as my place. Well, I wasn't home. I was down at this neighbor's, having them pick turkeys. I went down there and we had, oh, I don't know how many turkeys. Hundreds of them, okay. Well, I went down there there too and they brought her to my place. And when they got there, go by one of the, there you go. This is my brother, Mr. Vaughn. Oh, hi. He's from Spokane. Hello, Sarah. Good to meet you. You were walking there when I pulled out. Yeah, just when you pulled out. That's right. Yeah. I worked for the, for the county.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I was talking to her about the early days. Yeah. Well, she doesn't know about it. She really does. Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And so I was down to this woman down there, helping them with those turkeys. I had a whole crew. And we were down there and they called me. And this woman got over that far. And the neighbor, man, he wanted to bring it on, her on into town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, what do you want to do?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Kill that woman. And they stayed right there and she had a baby. Well, they just wrapped the baby up. And Dr. came to do her and everything. And when I got home, they called me and I went home. And there was that baby. I said, well, that's the first time there was a baby at Fry's house, but when she was home. But I took care of her for a few days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she stay at your house?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, she stayed right there.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And she went home again. And, but I, we often laughed about that because I, I'd rather pick turkeys, but I was done. I stayed there with her and to care her.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I'm up at night starting on that. So I was just from place to place. And so whatever they, and I'd go in most any place around whenever they needed someone, maybe harvest time or something like that. Well, I just helped with anything that was to be done. So that's the way my life went.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: It seems like to do that kind of work, though, there sure wasn't much money in it, though. No, there wasn't too much money, but then money was hard to get.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And I used to go whenever I could, so I could leave the children. One time our house caught a fire the night before we was waiting for a woman, that she was to have her baby, and the house caught a fire, and they came and got it out. Mr. Fry wasn't there. They came and got the fire out, and I just got, didn't even get it straightened up or anything, but the girls was big enough in. They went ahead then and straightened up the house, and then we had an old neighbor that used to come to our place so much. And he'd come down and stay with the children when they were there. He'd come down and stay, and they all liked it. And he was a carpenter, so he came right down. I went up and stayed with this woman to help take care of her and stayed with her. And this carpenter would come down and fix it, caught around the chimney, and they got it out, and of course lifted off a mesh. Of course, they used the water to do that. And he'd come down and fix that and stay with the children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a chimney fire?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah. No, it was a burnt -around, caught outside of the chimney, was burnt around, a burnt -hold around the chimney that they got about. The children were, they used to be a sawmill out from our place out there. Before I lived out in the schoolhouse, there was a sawmill right there. And there was over half a dozen or so youngsters out there that had had enough weenie feet. And I was sitting in the house. I didn't notice anything. Pretty soon he would come and couple up and they'd just pick the door just like fish, you know. And he said, Mama, our house is a fire. And he called us. About that time the doctor called and said, I'll be down by there 15 minutes. And he said, we're on our way. So we did. I just, I had my stuff ready to go. And then one time we made a trip up the Cedar Creek. That's when we had snow, too. Good many years ago. And so I said to the doctor now, I said, I don't know what we're getting into because I've never been to this place in my life. Well, that day they had piled the road. And of course when they first started fixing the road, they'd only piled. Just, this is when they had cars. And they'd just piled, just wide enough for a car to go. Well, if you got off of that where it had been kind of tramped down, you'd slide off here and you'd slide off there. Well, the doctor had to fight that car all the way up there. When he got to this place, he just, when he got in the house, he just loosened all of his clothes and he just wiped his face and neck and all. He was just ringing letters. And so the woman, well, she just, her baby all right, but it took all night. So we played cards till five o 'clock in the morning. And then the baby was born, the doctor took off and I stayed there in the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just you and the doctor played cards?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: No, she played too. Her and her husband and Will. And laugh, I never laughed so in my life because I forget the doctor was playing, I guess, with her husband. I think she was my partner. And he had kept all of his aces, you know. He didn't keep anything to say to those aces. And he played the wrong one the first time. That is, he played something that we got to lead and I led him out of his aces. Oh, we had Marpone over there because he was so sure he had all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The doctor?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah, the doctor was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What game was this that you were playing?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, Pinochro. Pinochro, yeah. And always had a lot of fun on my path. He sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you, when he was delivering the baby, what would you do? What would your job be for it?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I'd just be there handing the different things that he wanted.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And then as soon as the baby was born and he tied the card, then I'd have to take it and wash it and dress it. That was much fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he cool?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Never so excited in my life. And very, very nice to be with. He was just very nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there ever any difficult births that you had?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes, we had one, a daughter -in -law. She had a bed, but we had a nurse there and all with us. And she had a real hard time. And it took a long time for the baby to get here. And then after, it was over with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My son said to me, Mama, he says, How come that you ever took on anything like this?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: He says, I don't know how to understand it. I said, Well, I said, I don't like it. But I said, I always was paid for it, you know. So I hadn't got the money. So I was glad to get the money. And I'd go. But he didn't. That was a number he had each. It was more out after we got through because he just took every one of us and the one of us had first hold her and one another and then another. She had a hard time. And her mother was there. But she wouldn't say, She says, I'll go over and take care of the children. See, I lived right, they lived right close to me. And she says, I'll go over and cook for the children, take care of them if you would just take care of them. So we stayed with her over there. She took care of the children. It took quite a while, but she finally, but that was her first one. And she had a real hard time. And the other ones she had, she didn't have a bad time with them at all. She had, though she's got, she's got five. She has four boys and a girl. They're all grown now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like during the depression years for you and the family? Were you doing some of this stuff during that time, during the thirties? Was that a rough, a hard time?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I don't know. It was any harder than any other time, as far as I was concerned. We always took our grain. We had raised grain. And at that time, we would take our grain down to Patahaw down here and make, have it made into flour. And then we'd get our, all of course stuff and everything you know to use that we were used to using. We'd get all that stuff. Well, we had all of that stuff. And practically all we had to buy was coffee and sugar. Because we had our own flour. And then we had our own meat. And I canned lots of fruit. Just lots and lots of fruit. And I don't know if it was any harder than any other time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe there was things that we'd like to have, but we couldn't have it, so what was used to all of it?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: We had other things to eat, and we always had plenty to eat. The hardest thing we had one time when years ago, potatoes got up to five, six dollars a sack. And with all the children, you know, they liked potatoes. And Mr. Frans, he didn't like beans, but he ate them. We had beans. We ate beans, too. I don't like to eat it, but didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Five, six dollars, and how big a sack would that be? A hundred pounds?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yeah.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And it didn't take our family long to eat a hundred pounds because we all ate potatoes when we had them. But that was the hardest thing I think we had to get when we were kind of pretty short on money, was these potatoes.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But I had rice and other things that I used, but then you couldn't eat that all time.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pay did you get for this, the work in the camps and the logging camps?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, let's see. I got around...

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh,

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I'd say maybe five dollars a day. I don't know. Don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start doing that? Was that in the thirties? So you started cooking for them, or later?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Oh...

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I don't know. You still had kids, so... Oh, yes. Yes, the children. When I first started, I had been around town here then. I had been around town here working here in town.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But this was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the thirties must have been long before we came downhill, wasn't it?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Your kids...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old are your younger kids?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, my young ones are around 40.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: And the oldest ones, I think 65.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, it could have been the forties then, in there.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: But we never went hungry. That was one thing. We had... We had milk and we had butter and we had eggs. We got our coffee and we got our sugar. We didn't have a great lot of sugar, but we didn't suffer any for that. And then we had our own flour and our milk.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: You worked out so much. I'm just really impressed by how much you did in different places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like, you worked in town too at stores, eh?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes. When we first came downtown here, I worked in logs over here, and then I worked in bullets.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: I worked for... I think I worked for Marvin Long for 10 years. And then I worked for... blew it up here for 10 or 12 years. Then I went to the forestry and worked for about 12 years in the forestry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The forestry being what?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Up on Moscow Mountain was where I was...

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Well, I went from place to place. I was up on Moscow Mountain for several years. I was in that 10 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As a lookout?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Yes. Now that the lookout. I was a crew. See, the brush crew... I had anywhere from... Oh, sometimes I'd have two or three boys. Sometimes I'd have half a dozen. Then when they worked on the roads up there, I'd have extra men on that, or if they put somebody up there, put in some different things. So I'd have them cook for me. I had to cook for... cook... from the trailer house. First year or two, I was up there. They had a great big... one of these great big maroon -colored tents from the Army tanks. I cooked them out for the boys. And then I worked, I think, 10 years, 11 years, something like that. I worked for them, for the forestry. I had these boys.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: So... I loved that. I loved those kids, every one of them. A whole bunch of boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you do?

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Paper sack, no problem. Look in that door right there. See, there's one in there. If there isn't one out there in that room out behind there, you might find some. I burn up so many of them. I don't know what it's in here. There is one. Right out there, I think, on the floor somewhere. Look around. Dump something out if you can. So I know I've got kids around here that... boys that was on that forestry department that... we sure did. Dr. Christensen's boys, he had three boys. I cooked for them. They were on my cook. Tommy Dade and... and his brother. And old, the old one boy. There's a lot of boys around here. They all know him.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
words: Got you to be part of the... help in the community. I mean, helping other people. Seems like that's a lot of what you did. You wound up helping other people. Well, I did a lot of that as much as I could. Wherever I could help anybody, I'd go help them.

speaker: Frances Vaughan Fry
